video_title: Second-Half Market Outlook & Playbook with Liz Thomas
gist: Guy Adami and SoFi's Elizabeth Thomas preview bank earnings and the week's CPI/PPI prints, arguing that rising 10-year yields (near 4.6%) reflect Middle East-driven oil fears rather than an overheating economy that would justify Fed hikes, flag Japan's weakening yen (162) and deteriorating bond market as an underpriced risk, explain why Thomas exited her gold long on a broken thesis rather than a top call, and lay out a bullish-but-volatile second-half 2026 playbook favoring the Magnificent Seven, financials, healthcare, commodities, and energy.

insights.claim: Equity markets have been swinging through a rapid momentum-on/momentum-off pattern, with semiconductor stocks selling off sharply and then rebounding within short spans, which Elizabeth Thomas of SoFi views as a defining characteristic of the current market cycle likely to persist.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Investors should expect continued whipsaw volatility in momentum names like semis rather than assume any single directional trend will hold.
insights.quote: This sort of momentum on-momentum-off trade, where we've had semiconductors not doing really well, and then suddenly they rebound.

insights.claim: Money-center and investment banks have traded unusually well over the past few weeks despite a flattening yield curve that is normally a headwind for bank profitability, and Elizabeth Thomas will be watching Q2 earnings to see where that revenue strength is coming from and whether banks sound more constructive on capital-markets activity than on the yield curve itself.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: A yield-curve headwind that isn't showing up in bank stock performance suggests capital-markets/trading revenue could be the swing factor to watch in earnings reports.

insights.claim: Guy Adami observes that Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley have been trading with the kind of momentum and volatility usually associated with biotech stocks, raising the question of how durable that strength is heading into earnings.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Elevated, biotech-like volatility in bellwether bank stocks is a signal to watch for mean reversion or a durability test around earnings.
insights.quote: Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley feels like they're a biotech stock

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas argues that rising 10-year Treasury yields (approaching 4.6%) and 30-year yields are not being driven by an overheating economy that would justify Fed rate hikes, but by renewed Middle East conflict pushing oil prices back up from the low 70s toward the 90s, which is reviving inflation fears.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: If the yield rise is geopolitical/oil-driven rather than demand-driven, it argues against reading it as a signal the Fed needs to hike, and favors watching oil prices over growth data for the next leg in yields.
insights.quote: Yields, I believe, are rising because the conflict in the Middle East has reignited again

insights.claim: Both Guy Adami and Elizabeth Thomas believe the Federal Reserve will not hike rates this year, and see no strong case for a cut either, with Adami arguing the Fed could plausibly stay on hold until early next year or even next summer absent an extraordinary shock.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: A prolonged Fed hold, not just a pause before cuts, argues against positioning for imminent rate cuts and supports a higher-for-longer rate regime in portfolio construction.

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas points to CBO long-term debt projections over the next 5-10 years as terrifying, and argues that persistent above-target inflation combined with fiscal indiscipline on both sides of the aisle is causing the bond market to effectively doubt the Fed's stated commitment, under new chair Kevin Warsh following Jerome Powell, to getting inflation back to 2%.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Skepticism embedded in bond yields about the Fed hitting its 2% target argues for hedging inflation risk rather than assuming policy credibility is fully priced in.
insights.quote: it's almost as if the bond market is saying, "No, you won't."

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas notes that historically the bond market has been considered smarter than the stock market at sniffing out risk first, but across this entire cycle the bond market has repeatedly been proven wrong as equities kept prevailing, leaving open the question of whether the current rise in term premium means the bond market is finally right that risk is higher than equities are pricing.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: If this is the cycle where bonds are finally right, equity investors should watch rising term premium as an early warning rather than dismiss it as they have in prior false alarms.

insights.claim: Guy Adami notes that the bond market has historically challenged every incoming Fed chair regardless of who it is, and argues this dynamic, combined with the Middle East war, is creating a particularly difficult environment for new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Expect elevated bond-market volatility and credibility-testing moves specifically around Warsh's early tenure and policy communications.

insights.claim: June CPI is expected at 3.8%, down from 4.2% in May, with the print due Wednesday, and June PPI is expected at 6.2% following two prior monthly readings above 6%; Elizabeth Thomas believes CPI has likely already peaked if the Middle East conflict stays de-escalated and oil doesn't spike again, though any re-escalation in July would not yet show up in this particular reading.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: A sub-4% CPI print would support the peaked-inflation thesis and reduce pressure on the Fed, but the read is contingent on oil and Middle East de-escalation holding through July.

insights.claim: This CPI/PPI release is the first inflation data new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh will have a subsequent meeting to formally respond to, so Elizabeth Thomas expects it to be watched unusually closely, though she doesn't think it will materially change rate-hike expectations and still expects the market to price out hike risk later in the year.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Watch Warsh's post-meeting commentary following this data for the first real signal of his reaction function, which markets have not yet had a chance to price.

insights.claim: The yen has weakened to around 162 per dollar, a level near or beyond where the Bank of Japan has historically intervened around 160, yet as of this taping there's no clear sign of intervention, raising the question of whether the BOJ has quietly widened its tolerance band.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: A break of the BOJ's historical 160 intervention line without action would signal that policy tolerance has genuinely shifted, worth watching for confirmation via actual BOJ action.

insights.claim: Guy Adami flags that Japan's currency is weakening and its bond market deteriorating at what he calls a historic, alarming pace with almost no market attention, arguing the AI/semiconductor trade's indifference to Japan may be masking a real underlying risk rather than proving there isn't one.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Complacency toward Japan risk while AI/tech trades hold up could set up a sharper repricing if the yen or JGB market breaks decisively, similar to 2024.

insights.claim: Both hosts reference the July 2024 yen carry-trade unwind as a precedent: dollar-yen traded from 161 to below 158 within minutes around a CPI print, and by early August 2024 the VIX had spiked above 60, illustrating how quickly a Japan-driven shock can cascade into global volatility.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: The 2024 episode is the reference case for how fast a Japan/yen shock can transmit into a global volatility spike, useful as a tail-risk benchmark.

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas argues that if markets were correctly pricing the risk from Japan, US Treasury yields would be rising faster, as the BOJ would be forced to sell Treasuries to defend the yen, and the VIX, currently around 16.5, would not be so low, implying current pricing of Japan-related risk is complacent.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: A low VIX alongside a deteriorating yen and JGB market is a mispricing signal; a spike in either could be an early tell that the market is finally waking up to Japan risk.
insights.quote: I think the VIX at 16 and a half wherever it's currently trading is completely mispriced

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas estimates Japan is the single largest foreign holder of US Treasuries but only around 13% of total outstanding Treasuries, so while a forced unwind to defend the yen would pressure Treasury yields, it would not be a majority-holder-scale shock; she believes the market is currently right to look past Japan risk for now, though the underlying vulnerabilities of debt-to-GDP above 200%, demographics, and currency and bond-market stress haven't disappeared.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Japan risk is a slow-burn tail risk rather than an imminent trigger at current yen levels, but a move meaningfully beyond 160 would warrant reassessment.

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas attributes the recent renewed sell-off in private-equity-adjacent names, which had sold off hard in spring, bounced, and are now reversing that bounce, to idiosyncratic and technical factors such as an oversold rebound unwinding and rotation out of momentum trades, rather than a genuine credit-cycle warning, since high-yield and investment-grade credit spreads remain tight and aren't widening.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Tight, non-widening credit spreads argue investors shouldn't extrapolate private-equity stock weakness into a broader credit-cycle call yet.

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas suggests a wave of debt issuance from mega-cap tech companies including Amazon and Google in public markets may be satisfying investor demand for AI/technology credit exposure that previously flowed into private credit, reducing the need for private-market exposure and potentially shaking out weaker holders, creating an entry opportunity for more fundamental long-term buyers in private names.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Public AI-linked debt issuance could structurally compete with private credit for investor dollars, a mechanism to watch for continued pressure on private-credit-exposed equities.

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas believes classic credit cycles have been largely suppressed in recent years because the Federal Reserve's explicit focus on financial stability leads it to intervene whenever stress starts building in debt markets, though she still expects a genuine credit cycle to eventually occur, possibly in a less severe form than historically.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: As long as the Fed prioritizes financial stability, investors should expect credit stress to be repeatedly capped rather than allowed to cascade, until a shock large enough to overwhelm that backstop occurs.
insights.quote: Anytime something starts to inch towards a problem, it gets saved.

insights.claim: Guy Adami speculates, without direct knowledge, that the current administration may take a more old-school, laissez-faire approach that lets credit and market stress play out more naturally rather than providing as robust a financial safety net as prior administrations.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: A less interventionist policy stance toward emerging credit stress would raise the tail risk of an actual credit cycle materializing compared to the recent always-gets-saved pattern.

themes.theme: Gold: Thesis Break, Not a Top Call

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas says she exited her gold long near the top a couple of months ago not because she predicted the top, but because gold's price action stopped behaving in line with her original thesis, and once she could no longer explain the move she chose to exit.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Her framework is a useful discipline for any trade: exit on loss of thesis-conviction rather than trying to precisely call a top.
insights.quote: I don't understand what's going on here anymore. What I thought was going to be happening isn't, so I'm out.

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas says gold's earlier rally was driven by sustained central-bank buying and then went parabolic in January as retail investors piled in on FOMO, visible in ETF holdings rising in lockstep with the gold price; both central-bank buying and retail ETF flows have since unwound, which is what stalled the rally.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Gold ETF holdings are a real-time proxy for the retail-flow driver of the rally; their continued decline would confirm the thesis remains broken.

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas believes central banks, particularly in Asia, stepped back from active gold buying from March through June because the war forced them to raise liquidity to pay for higher oil import costs, an under-the-radar mechanism linking the Middle East conflict to gold's stall.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: If oil prices stay elevated on renewed conflict, it could keep central banks as liquidity-constrained buyers of gold, a secondary channel to watch alongside the direct inflation effect of oil.

insights.claim: For gold to reclaim its prior highs, Elizabeth Thomas says either central banks need to resume active buying or retail needs to flood back in at scale, but she thinks gold has become too volatile for retail investors to feel comfortable re-entering right now; she still views gold as a viable long-term diversifier, just not currently behaving as one.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Track central-bank gold purchase data and gold ETF flows as the two triggers that would need to reverse before re-entering a gold long.

insights.claim: Guy Adami maintains a long-held, and so far incorrect by his own admission, view that the historically negative relationship between a strong dollar plus rising US yields and gold will eventually flip, turning what has been a headwind into a tailwind for gold.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Even a strategist who has been wrong on timing continuing to hold this view is worth tracking as a potential contrarian catalyst if the dollar/yield-gold relationship inflects.

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas is directionally bullish that equities end the second half of 2026 higher, but expects high-velocity, high-volatility moves beneath the index level given upcoming midterm elections and a momentum trade that has already shown cracks.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: A bullish index call paired with expected high dispersion argues for active stock and sector selection over passive index exposure into year-end.

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas expects the Magnificent Seven, which lagged so badly in the first half that some investors nicknamed it the Leg 7, to rotate back into market leadership in the second half, driven by investors returning to familiar large-cap stalwarts during volatility and by improved relative valuations after the momentum unwind.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: A rotation back into mega-cap tech leadership would favor large-cap growth exposure over the broader momentum trade for second-half positioning.
insights.quote: People started calling it the leg 7 for the first half of the year

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas expects financials to continue performing well in the second half given their resilience through a flattening yield curve, and will be watching upcoming bank earnings closely for confirmation.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Continued bank-stock strength despite a yield-curve headwind supports staying overweight financials into earnings season.

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas has a high-conviction call on healthcare for the second half, framing it as both a beneficiary of investor demand for growth exposure and a historically strong-performing, defensive sector specifically during mid-term election years.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Healthcare offers a combination of growth optionality and election-year defensiveness that Thomas considers a standout sector allocation for the second half.
insights.quote: Healthcare, I have pounded the table on viciously.

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas and her colleague Mario argue that standard portfolio construction using an efficient frontier built on data back to the 1970s is misleading now because that dataset spans a 40-year secular period of falling rates and a bond bull market that no longer applies, and their updated work shows commodities are a genuinely effective diversifier in this new inflation and yield regime.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Investors relying on historical efficient-frontier optimization may be structurally underweight commodities for the current higher-inflation, higher-yield regime.
insights.quote: We can't look at an efficient frontier and use the entire data set back to the 1970s because we had a 40-year period in there where interest rates were falling and bonds were bullish.

insights.claim: Elizabeth Thomas views the recent de-escalation of the Middle East conflict as having created a buying opportunity in the energy sector, and expects energy to perform well in the second half despite ongoing war-related uncertainty.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: A de-escalation-driven pullback in energy names is framed as a dip to buy rather than a reason to avoid the sector.
insights.quote: I think actually de-escalation of the war has provided a buying opportunity.

insights.claim: Guy Adami notes that even though crude oil prices have pulled back significantly from their high of a month and a half to two months earlier, refiners like Valero and Marathon Petroleum have continued making new all-time highs, an idiosyncratic divergence within the energy trade.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Refiners diverging positively from crude-oil weakness suggests refining margins or idiosyncratic factors are currently more important than crude direction for that sub-sector.
insights.quote: names like Valero, Marathon Petroleum continue to make new all-time highs
